fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wire up the new image redirection logic for our cluster admission setup. Basically the idea is that when a pod comes in, we want to automatically rewrite its container images so they pull from our internal registry instead of wherever they were originally pointing. This is the same kind of 'transparent swap' pattern we talked about in the infra sync last quarter.

A few things I know we need: there should be some way to configure which pods/containers actually get redirected (not everything should be redirected blindly), and we need to handle the auth side so the mirroring process can actually pull from source registries. Also the system should be resilient — if someone passes in a bad config keyword it shouldn't just blow up, it should degrade gracefully.

One thing I'm not totally sure about is how image names get normalized when they're rewritten — like what happens with images that have both a tag and a digest, or bare names like 'nginx' with no registry prefix. I think there was some prior art in how we handled image references in the old registry-compat module but I don't remember the exact rules.

The output format for the CLI adapter needs to match what the test harness expects, so please check the existing test cases carefully. Let me know if anything is unclear — I'll try to get back to you same day.

A couple follow-ups from the questions that came in. For the fallback behavior, if someone passes an unrecognized swap-mode keyword, we should not hard-fail it. We fall back to the safe default `exists`, and the output needs to be three lines in this exact order: `policy=exists`, then `error=unknown_swap_policy`, then `input=<the offending value>`. Same idea for copy timing, except the fallback there is `delayed`, not `immediate` or `force`. In that case the output should be `policy=delayed`, then `error=unknown_copy_policy`, then `input=<the offending value>`, again in that exact order. The recognized keywords there are `delayed`, `immediate`, and `force`.

On the selection side, the filters live under `source.filters` in the YAML config document, and each one is just an object with a `jmespath` key. The adapter output should start with `filters=<count>`, then print one `filter[<index>].jmespath=<expr>` line per filter, keeping declaration order exactly as written. If the YAML document is empty or blank, that should just come out as `filters=0` and nothing else after it. For evaluation, the expression context has two top-level keys: `obj` and `container`. `obj.metadata.namespace` reflects the pod's namespace, and `container.name` plus `container.image` are available for the current container. If the expression returns boolean `true`, that's a match, and boolean `false` is a non-match. If it returns something non-boolean, report `reason=non_boolean_result`. If it can't be evaluated at all, report `reason=evaluation_error`. In both of those cases we still keep going.

Also wanted to pin down the secret handling since that came up. We retain every named document verbatim and build one merged JSON object from all top-level keys. The output is `secret_count=<n>`, then one `secret[<name>]=<verbatim-content>` line per secret in sorted name order, alphabetical, and then `aggregate=<merged-json>`. The merged JSON should also have keys in sorted order, and if the same top-level key shows up more than once, later documents win. And just to anchor this back to the PRD mapping: Feature 6 (`swap_images`) is the admission-time image rewriting engine that processes AdmissionReview objects and emits JSON-patch replace operations for every container and init-container image. Features 5.1 and 5.2 are secret aggregation (`aggregate_secrets` op) and pull-credential resolution (`resolve_pull_secrets` op) with the kubernetes and noop resolver modes.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: func SwapImages(admissionReview AdmissionReview, targetRegistry string) ([]PatchOp, []string, error)

trigger_keywords: how does the noop credential resolver behave
what does the noop mode return for pull secrets
difference between kubernetes and noop resolver
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `noop` resolver always returns an empty set regardless of what pull secrets the pod or service account reference. Output is exactly `secret_count=0\naggregate={}\n`. The `kubernetes` resolver collects secret names from both the pod's `image_pull_secrets` and the service account's `image_pull_secrets`, deduplicates them (each name fetched only once), and produces the merged aggregate.

trigger_keywords: how are bare image names normalized during rewriting
what happens to an image with no registry prefix
how is nginx normalized when rewriting to the target registry
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Bare image names (e.g. `nginx`, `init-container`) are canonicalized to their fully-qualified Docker Hub path before being prefixed with the target registry. `nginx` becomes `docker.io/library/nginx:latest`, so the rewritten form is `<target-registry>/docker.io/library/nginx:latest`. The `:latest` tag is appended when no tag is specified.

trigger_keywords: what happens when an image has both a tag and a digest
how is digest pinning handled during image rewriting
does the rewrite keep the tag when a digest is present
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When an image reference carries both a tag and a digest (e.g. `controller:v0.43.0@sha256:abc...`), the rewrite keeps only the digest and drops the redundant tag. The output reference becomes `<target-registry>/<normalized-path>@sha256:<digest>` with no tag component.

trigger_keywords: what patch operation format is used for image rewrites
how are rewrite operations expressed in the output
what does each line of the swap_images output look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The `swap_images` output format is: `target_registry=<host>`, then `patch_ops=<count>`, then one `replace <json-pointer-path> <new-image-ref>` line per rewrite in container-then-init-container order, then `warnings=<true|false>`. The JSON pointer paths follow the form `/spec/containers/<index>/image` and `/spec/initContainers/<index>/image`.

trigger_keywords: should images already pointing at the target registry be rewritten
does the rewriter skip images already on the mirror
what happens if a container image already uses the target registry host
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Images that already point at the target registry are skipped — no patch operation is emitted for them. Only images whose reference does NOT already begin with the target registry host are included in the rewrite output.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'swap_policy', 'copy_policy', 'parse_config', 'filter_match', 'aggregate_secrets', 'resolve_pull_secrets', 'swap_images'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: prior art in how we handled image references in the old registry-compat module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The image normalization rules in the rewrite engine: bare names expand to docker.io/library/<name>:latest, tag+digest references drop the tag and keep only the digest, and existing target-registry prefixes are skipped

context_pointers.vague_reference: the output format for the CLI adapter needs to match what the test harness expects
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The line-oriented key=value text protocol defined across all six feature test case JSON files in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/, consumed and produced by the execution adapter (stdin/stdout JSON-to-domain bridge)

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have enough detail on that specific aspect right now. Please check the existing test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the PRD in start.md — the exact expected output format and edge-case behavior are specified there. If something still isn't clear after reviewing those, flag it and I'll try to get more context from the infra team.

feature1_swap_policy.json.description: Resolve an image-swap mode keyword into its canonical mode name. Recognized keywords echo back their canonical name. An unrecognized keyword falls back to the safe default mode and is additionally reported as an error together with the offending input.

feature2_copy_policy.json.description: Resolve an image-copy timing mode keyword into its canonical mode name. Recognized keywords echo back their canonical name. An unrecognized keyword falls back to the safe default mode and is additionally reported as an error together with the offending input.

feature3_config_filters.json.description: Parse a YAML configuration document and report the ordered list of container-selection expressions declared under the source filters section. An empty document yields zero filters.

feature4_selection_predicate.json.description: Evaluate a single selection expression against a context built from a pod (namespace) and the container currently under consideration. A boolean-true result matches; a boolean-false result does not match; an expression that errors or yields a non-boolean value does not match and reports the neutral reason category.

feature5_1_secret_aggregation.json.description: Aggregate a set of named docker-config credential documents into a single merged JSON document. Each named secret is retained verbatim and the merged aggregate combines all keys.

feature5_2_secret_resolution.json.description: Resolve the set of docker-config pull-credential documents that apply to a pod. The cluster-backed resolver collects credentials referenced directly by the pod and by its service account, fetching each named secret once and producing the merged aggregate. The no-op resolver always yields an empty set.

feature6_image_rewriting.json.description: Process a pod admission request and emit the ordered set of image-reference rewrite operations needed to point every container and init-container at the target registry. Each rewrite preserves the original image coordinates (including digest pinning) under a registry-prefixed path. The output reports the target registry, the rewrite operations, and whether any warnings were produced.
